stories.title: PM Wong Picks His New Team of Leaders
stories.headline_kid: Singapore's Prime Minister has chosen a fresh group of ministers to help steer the country for years to come.
summary: Imagine your class is choosing a new set of monitors and helpers for the rest of the year. Prime Minister Lawrence Wong just did something similar, but for the whole country. He announced a new Cabinet — the team of top ministers who help him run Singapore.

What's interesting is that several Members of Parliament who were only elected for the first time have been quickly given big, important jobs. This is called being 'fast-tracked', like a student skipping straight to captain because the coach already trusts their skills.

PM Wong says he wants a team he can trust and work closely with, one that can carry Singapore forward for the next few decades, not just the next few years. Building a strong team early means the country is ready for whatever challenges come next.

This matters because Singapore's leaders make decisions about things like school policies, healthcare, jobs and how our country deals with the rest of the world. A well-chosen team helps keep Singapore stable and moving in a good direction.

lenses.me: The ministers PM Wong picked today could be making decisions about your school syllabus, CCA funding or even hawker food prices in the years ahead.
lenses.family: Your parents may talk about which minister is now in charge of things like housing, healthcare or transport that affect your family's daily life.
lenses.friends: You and your classmates might discuss this in Social Studies lessons about how Singapore's government works and changes.
lenses.nation: It shows how Singapore renews its leadership smoothly, without chaos, which is one reason our country stays stable and trusted.
lenses.world: Other countries often study Singapore's calm leadership handovers as an example of good governance.

compo_words.meaning: when people are moved into new jobs or positions within a team
compo_words.example: The coach announced a reshuffle of the school football team before the big match.

compo_words.meaning: to give someone a responsibility because you trust them
compo_words.example: The teacher entrusted Mei Ling with organising the class party.

compo_words.meaning: very important, especially because of future effects
compo_words.example: Choosing a new class leader was a momentous decision for our group project.

compo_phrases.meaning: to lead a nation into the future
compo_phrases.example: The new ministers hope to carry the country forward with fresh ideas.

compo_phrases.meaning: to start something quickly and effectively
compo_phrases.example: The new MP had to hit the ground running in his first week as minister.

questions: Why do you think it is important for a country to sometimes bring in new, younger leaders?
If you were chosen to lead your class for a project, how would you build trust with your teammates?
Do you think it's fair to give someone a big responsibility soon after they join a team? Why or why not?
phrases: I think this is important because ...
One reason for this could be ...

stories.title: Secret Tip-Offs Help Catch Cheaters in Singapore
stories.headline_kid: Brave people who report wrongdoing without giving their names are helping Singapore fight corruption.
summary: Corruption is when someone secretly gives or takes money to break rules unfairly — like a referee who accepts a bribe to help one football team win. In Singapore, an agency called CPIB (Corrupt Practices Investigation Bureau) exists to catch people who do this.

Last year, about 35 per cent of the reports CPIB received were anonymous, meaning the person who reported it didn't give their name. Nearly a quarter of all investigations started because of these brave, anonymous tip-offs.

CPIB officers explain that people are often too scared to report corruption openly, especially if it involves their boss or someone powerful. Anonymous reporting lets ordinary people speak up safely, like dropping a secret note into a suggestion box instead of raising your hand in front of everyone.

This matters because corruption, even small amounts, can be like a tiny crack in a wall — if ignored, it grows bigger and can hurt trust in the whole system. Singapore is known worldwide for being one of the least corrupt countries, and these tip-offs help keep it that way.

lenses.me: It teaches you that being honest matters, and that speaking up about unfairness — even quietly — can make a real difference.
lenses.family: Your parents' workplaces depend on fair rules; corruption could mean unfair promotions or unsafe shortcuts being taken.
lenses.friends: It's similar to telling a teacher quietly if someone is cheating in an exam, instead of staying silent.
lenses.nation: Singapore's reputation for being clean and trustworthy attracts businesses and jobs from around the world.
lenses.world: Countries with high corruption often struggle with poverty and unfair systems, so Singapore's example matters globally.

compo_words.meaning: dishonest behaviour, especially involving bribery, by people in power
compo_words.example: The government works hard to stamp out corruption in public offices.
She left an anonymous note in the class suggestion box.

compo_words.meaning: the quality of being honest and having strong moral principles
compo_words.example: The student was praised for his integrity when he returned the lost wallet.

compo_phrases.meaning: to stop a problem before it grows bigger
compo_phrases.example: Reporting small dishonest acts early helps nip corruption in the bud.

compo_phrases.meaning: to report wrongdoing to the authorities
compo_phrases.example: He decided to blow the whistle on his colleague's dishonest dealings.

questions: If you saw a classmate cheating, would you tell the teacher openly or anonymously? Why?
Why do you think some people are afraid to report wrongdoing even when they know it's wrong?
How do you think honesty helps build trust between friends?
phrases: If I were in that situation, I would ...
One way to solve this is ...

stories.title: Oil Prices Racing Toward $100 as Middle East Tensions Rise
stories.headline_kid: Fighting far away between the US and Iran is pushing oil prices up fast, and that could make everyday things pricier for Singapore families.
summary: Oil is like the fuel that powers much of our world — it's turned into petrol for cars, fuel for planes, and even used to make plastic. A key type of oil called Brent crude just jumped above US$95 for one barrel, and some experts think it could hit US$100 soon.

Why the sudden rise? Tensions between the US and Iran have been getting worse, and there are fears that fighting could block the Strait of Hormuz — a narrow, crowded sea passage where a huge share of the world's oil ships pass through. If that route gets blocked, less oil reaches the rest of the world.

When oil becomes scarce or people worry it might become scarce, its price shoots up — a bit like how bottled water becomes expensive right after a big storm warning. Higher oil prices can make petrol, cooking gas, flights and even the cost of transporting food more expensive.

Singapore doesn't have its own oil, so we buy all of it from other countries. This means price shocks happening thousands of kilometres away in the Middle East can still be felt here, in things like taxi fares, electricity bills, and prices at the supermarket.

lenses.me: Bus and taxi fares, or even your family's Grab rides, could slowly become more expensive if oil prices stay high.
lenses.family: Your family's petrol costs, cooking gas and electricity bills might rise, making household budgeting tighter.
lenses.friends: Your friends' families who drive to school might notice fuel costs creeping up too.
lenses.nation: Since Singapore imports 100% of its oil, our country is very sensitive to price swings caused by conflicts overseas.
lenses.world: Rising oil prices affect nearly every country's economy, since almost everything — from factories to farming — needs energy to run.

compo_words.meaning: a standard unit used to measure amounts of oil
compo_words.example: The price of one barrel of oil rose sharply this week.

compo_words.meaning: to become more serious or intense
compo_words.example: The conflict began to escalate, worrying oil traders around the world.

compo_words.meaning: likely to change suddenly and unpredictably
compo_words.example: Oil prices have become very volatile because of the ongoing tensions.

compo_phrases.meaning: when one event causes a chain of other effects
compo_phrases.example: The oil price hike had a ripple effect on transport costs everywhere.

compo_phrases.meaning: to spend less money because times are hard
compo_phrases.example: Families may need to tighten their belts if fuel prices keep rising.

questions: Have you noticed your family talking about rising prices at home? How did that make you feel?
Why do you think events happening far away, like in the Middle East, can affect prices in Singapore?
What are some ways your family could save money if fuel prices go up?
phrases: In my opinion, my family could ...
One thing we could do is ...

sources.name: CNA - Oil prices jump on Mideast war

stories.title: Leaders Meet in Manila to Talk About Peace at Sea
stories.headline_kid: Top diplomats from around the world gathered in the Philippines to try to keep the busy South China Sea peaceful.
summary: The South China Sea is one of the busiest and most important seas in the world — huge ships carrying goods like electronics, toys and food sail through it every day. But several countries claim parts of this sea, and tensions recently flared up again near the Philippines.

This week, foreign ministers from ASEAN — the group of 10 Southeast Asian countries that includes Singapore — met in Manila to discuss a 'code of conduct'. Think of it like traffic rules for the sea, meant to stop ships and navies from bumping into each other or fighting.

At the same meeting, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio spoke with China's top diplomat, Wang Yi. They discussed plans for Chinese President Xi Jinping to visit Washington in September, showing that even amid tension, the two superpowers are still talking to each other.

This matters to Singapore because our country relies heavily on the sea for trade — many of the goods we use every day travel by ship through this region. Keeping the seas calm and rules-based helps protect trade routes that Singapore and the world depend on.

lenses.me: Many of your toys, gadgets and even school supplies likely travelled through this sea on a cargo ship to reach Singapore.
lenses.family: If shipping routes get disrupted, the cost and availability of imported goods your family buys could be affected.
lenses.friends: This is a great real-world example for Social Studies about how Southeast Asian countries work together through ASEAN.
lenses.nation: Singapore, as an ASEAN member and major port, has a strong interest in keeping regional seas peaceful and open for trade.
lenses.world: Tensions between major powers like the US and China affect global stability, trade and even prices of everyday goods worldwide.

compo_words.meaning: someone who represents their country in dealing with other countries
compo_words.example: The diplomat travelled to Manila to discuss important issues with other nations.

compo_words.meaning: a disagreement, especially over rights or claims
compo_words.example: The territorial dispute over the sea has lasted many years.

compo_words.meaning: a feeling of unease or conflict between groups
compo_words.example: Tension between the two countries grew after the incident at sea.

compo_phrases.meaning: to prepare the basic conditions needed for something to happen
compo_phrases.example: The two ministers met to lay the groundwork for a future visit.
ASEAN leaders worked hard to keep the peace in the region.

questions: Why do you think it's important for countries to have rules, even for something like the sea?
Can you think of a time you had a disagreement with a friend that needed 'rules' to solve fairly?
Why might it matter to Singapore if a sea far from us becomes unsafe for ships?
phrases: From what I know, Singapore relies on ...
In my view, the best solution would be ...
One consequence of this could be ...

sources.name: CNA - South China Sea tensions and ASEAN talks

stories.title: Living With Pain Every Day, But Never Giving Up
stories.headline_kid: Meet a young Singaporean who deals with pain every single day but refuses to just 'get by' — she wants to truly live her life.
summary: Most of us have felt pain from a scraped knee or a bad cold, and it goes away after a while. But some people live with pain that never fully disappears — this is called chronic pain, and it can last for months or even years.

Nay Chi, a 25-year-old in Singapore, has several long-term health conditions that cause her constant pain. She sometimes needs a walking stick just to move around, and her conditions have affected her studies, her work, and even her relationships with family.

Her story is featured in a CNA documentary called 'On The Red Dot'. Many everyday decisions that most people don't think twice about — like whether to take the stairs or how long they can stand in a queue — are things Nay Chi has to carefully plan around every day.

Despite these struggles, Nay Chi says she doesn't just want to 'survive' — she wants to do more with her life. Her courage reminds us that people with invisible illnesses often carry heavy burdens quietly, and a little kindness and understanding can go a long way.

lenses.me: It reminds you that some classmates might be facing hidden struggles, and being patient and kind with them really matters.
lenses.family: It's a chance to appreciate your own family's health and to be understanding if a family member is unwell.
lenses.friends: You might think of ways to include friends who have health challenges in games or activities, without making them feel left out.
lenses.nation: Singapore is becoming more aware of 'invisible' illnesses, encouraging workplaces and schools to be more inclusive.
lenses.world: Chronic illness affects millions of people worldwide, and Nay Chi's story is part of a bigger global conversation about empathy and accessibility.

compo_words.meaning: lasting for a long time, often referring to an illness
compo_words.example: She has learnt to manage her chronic pain with the help of doctors.

compo_words.meaning: the ability to recover quickly from difficulties
compo_words.example: Her resilience inspired everyone who heard her story.

compo_words.meaning: the ability to understand and share the feelings of others
compo_words.example: Treating others with empathy makes the world kinder.

compo_phrases.example: Even in pain, she kept pushing forward to achieve her goals.

compo_phrases.meaning: to imagine what it's like to experience someone else's situation
compo_phrases.example: We should try to walk in her shoes before judging her choices.

questions: How would you feel if a friend had a health condition that wasn't visible to others?
What could you do to help a classmate who struggles with health issues at school?
Why do you think it's important to be understanding towards people facing hidden difficulties?
phrases: If I were in her situation, I would ...
One way I could help is ...

sources.name: CNA - Nay Chi chronic pain story
